--- title: "Bikeshare SpaceTime Prediction" subtitle: "CPLN 5080 - Spring 2026" author: "Veronica Zheng" date: today format: html: code-fold: true code-tools: true toc: true toc-depth: 3 toc-location: left theme: cosmo embed-resources: true editor: visual execute: warning: false message: false --- # 1. Introduction This homework extends the Q1 2025 space-time prediction workflow to **Q2 2025 (April–June)**, Philadelphia's spring-to-early-summer transition period. Compared to Q1's cold winter months, Q2 brings warmer temperatures, longer daylight hours, and major shifts in rider behavior — making it an interesting contrast for evaluating how well our models generalize across seasons. Q2 also captures several demand-shaping events unique to spring in Philadelphia: the end of university semesters (Penn, Drexel, Temple), increasing recreational riding on weekends, and the onset of outdoor event season. These factors motivate the new features added. This analysis follows the same five-model framework introduced in the in-class Q1 walkthrough, with the following additions: - A direct **MAE comparison between Q1 and Q2** to assess seasonal model performance differences - Deeper **error analysis** across space, time, and demographic dimensions - **Three new engineered features** informed by Q2-specific demand patterns - A **Poisson regression** alternative to OLS for count data - A **critical reflection** on operational deployment and equity implications # 2. In-Class Q1 Results ## Load and Process Indego Trip Data (Q1 2025) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Read Q1 2025 data indego <- read_csv(here("data/indego-trips-2025-q1.csv")) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # How many trips? cat("Total trips in Q1 2025:", nrow(indego), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Total trips in Q1 2025: 201588 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Date range cat("Date range:", min(mdy_hm(indego$start_time)), "to", max(mdy_hm(indego$start_time)), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Date range: 1735689600 to 1743464880 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # How many unique stations? cat("Unique start stations:", length(unique(indego$start_station)), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Unique start stations: 265 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Trip types table(indego$trip_route_category) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` One Way Round Trip 190792 10796 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Passholder types table(indego$passholder_type) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Day Pass Indego30 Indego365 IndegoFlex Walk-up 5494 94044 91628 3 10419 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Bike types table(indego$bike_type) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` electric standard 129561 72027 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} indego <- indego %>% mutate( # Parse datetime start_datetime = mdy_hm(start_time), end_datetime = mdy_hm(end_time), # Create hourly bins interval60 = floor_date(start_datetime, unit = "hour"), # Extract time features week = week(interval60), month = month(interval60, label = TRUE), dotw = wday(interval60, label = TRUE), hour = hour(interval60), date = as.Date(interval60), # Create useful indicators weekend = ifelse(dotw %in% c("Sat", "Sun"), 1, 0), rush_hour = ifelse(hour %in% c(7, 8, 9, 16, 17, 18), 1, 0) ) # Look at temporal features head(indego %>% select(start_datetime, interval60, week, dotw, hour, weekend)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 6 × 6 start_datetime interval60 week dotw hour weekend 1 2025-01-01 00:00:00 2025-01-01 00:00:00 1 Wed 0 0 2 2025-01-01 00:04:00 2025-01-01 00:00:00 1 Wed 0 0 3 2025-01-01 00:05:00 2025-01-01 00:00:00 1 Wed 0 0 4 2025-01-01 00:05:00 2025-01-01 00:00:00 1 Wed 0 0 5 2025-01-01 00:08:00 2025-01-01 00:00:00 1 Wed 0 0 6 2025-01-01 00:14:00 2025-01-01 00:00:00 1 Wed 0 0 ``` ::: ::: `interval60` rounds each trip's start timestamp down to the nearest hour using `floor_date()`. For example, a trip that started at 8:47 AM gets assigned to the 8:00 AM bin, and a trip at 8:03 AM also gets assigned to 8:00 AM. This creates a consistent hourly time unit that lets us aggregate all trips happening within the same hour at the same station into a single count, which is the foundation of our space-time panel structure. ### Daily Trip ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Daily trip counts daily_trips <- indego %>% group_by(date) %>% summarize(trips = n()) ggplot(daily_trips, aes(x = date, y = trips)) + geom_line(color = "#3182bd", linewidth = 1) + geom_smooth(se = FALSE, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") + labs( title = "Indego Daily Ridership - Q1 2025", subtitle = "Winter demand patterns in Philadelphia", x = "Date", y = "Daily Trips", caption = "Source: Indego bike share" ) + plotTheme ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](week11_inclass_and_lab5_files/figure-html/trips_over_time-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: Ridership shows a clear upward trend from January through March, rising from roughly 1,500 trips per day in early January to over 3,500 by late March, reflecting the seasonal effect of warming temperatures making cycling more appealing. There is also a strong weekly cyclical pattern throughout the quarter, with regular peaks and troughs corresponding to weekdays versus weekends. The most notable anomaly is the sharp spike on February 14th, which far exceeds any typical Friday ridership — this was the day of the Philadelphia Eagles Super Bowl victory parade, which drew massive crowds downtown and drove an unusual surge in bike share usage. ### Hourly Patterns ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Average trips by hour and day type hourly_patterns <- indego %>% group_by(hour, weekend) %>% summarize(avg_trips = n() / n_distinct(date)) %>% mutate(day_type = ifelse(weekend == 1, "Weekend", "Weekday")) ggplot(hourly_patterns, aes(x = hour, y = avg_trips, color = day_type)) + geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) + scale_color_manual(values = c("Weekday" = "#08519c", "Weekend" = "#6baed6")) + labs( title = "Average Hourly Ridership Patterns", subtitle = "Clear commute patterns on weekdays", x = "Hour of Day", y = "Average Trips per Hour", color = "Day Type" ) + plotTheme ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](week11_inclass_and_lab5_files/figure-html/hourly_patterns-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: Weekdays show two sharp peaks at hour 8 (morning rush, \~210 trips) and hour 17 (evening rush, \~270 trips), with a notable dip in between, reflecting classic commuter behavior. Weekends show a very different pattern with no morning spike, instead rising gradually from mid-morning and plateauing broadly between hours 12 and 17 at around 150 trips, suggesting recreational rather than commute-driven usage. Overall, weekdays have higher peak demand but weekends sustain more consistent midday ridership. ### Top Stations ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Most popular origin stations top_stations <- indego %>% count(start_station, start_lat, start_lon, name = "trips") %>% arrange(desc(trips)) %>% head(20) kable(top_stations, caption = "Top 20 Indego Stations by Trip Origins", format.args = list(big.mark = ",")) %>% kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover")) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}

````` ::: ::: ## Load Philadelphia Census Data ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Get Philadelphia census tracts philly_census <- get_acs( geography = "tract", variables = c( "B01003_001", # Total population "B19013_001", # Median household income "B08301_001", # Total commuters "B08301_010", # Commute by transit "B02001_002", # White alone "B25077_001" # Median home value ), state = "PA", county = "Philadelphia", year = 2022, geometry = TRUE, output = "wide" ) %>% rename( Total_Pop = B01003_001E, Med_Inc = B19013_001E, Total_Commuters = B08301_001E, Transit_Commuters = B08301_010E, White_Pop = B02001_002E, Med_Home_Value = B25077_001E ) %>% mutate( Percent_Taking_Transit = (Transit_Commuters / Total_Commuters) * 100, Percent_White = (White_Pop / Total_Pop) * 100 ) %>% st_transform(crs = 4326) #transforming to WGS84 for lat/lon matching, currently in NAD83 ``` ::: ### Map Philadelphia Context ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Map median income ggplot() + geom_sf(data = philly_census, aes(fill = Med_Inc), color = NA) + scale_fill_viridis( option = "viridis", name = "Median\nIncome", labels = scales::dollar ) + labs( title = "Philadelphia Median Household Income by Census Tract", subtitle = "Context for understanding bike share demand patterns" ) + # Stations geom_point( data = indego, aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat), color = "red", size = 0.25, alpha = 0.6 ) + mapTheme ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](week11_inclass_and_lab5_files/figure-html/map_philly-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ### Join Census Data to Stations ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Create sf object for stations stations_sf <- indego %>% distinct(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>% filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon)) %>% st_as_sf(coords = c("start_lon", "start_lat"), crs = 4326) # Spatial join to get census tract for each station stations_census <- st_join(stations_sf, philly_census, left = TRUE) %>% st_drop_geometry() # Look at the result - investigate whether all of the stations joined to census data -- according to the map above there are stations in non-residential tracts. stations_for_map <- indego %>% distinct(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>% filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon)) %>% left_join( stations_census %>% select(start_station, Med_Inc), by = "start_station" ) %>% mutate(has_census = !is.na(Med_Inc)) # Add back to trip data indego_census <- indego %>% left_join( stations_census %>% select(start_station, Med_Inc, Percent_Taking_Transit, Percent_White, Total_Pop), by = "start_station" ) # Prepare data for visualization stations_for_map <- indego %>% distinct(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>% filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon)) %>% left_join( stations_census %>% select(start_station, Med_Inc), by = "start_station" ) %>% mutate(has_census = !is.na(Med_Inc)) # Create the map showing problem stations ggplot() + geom_sf(data = philly_census, aes(fill = Med_Inc), color = "white", size = 0.1) + scale_fill_viridis( option = "viridis", name = "Median\nIncome", labels = scales::dollar, na.value = "grey90" ) + # Stations with census data (small grey dots) geom_point( data = stations_for_map %>% filter(has_census), aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat), color = "grey30", size = 1, alpha = 0.6 ) + # Stations WITHOUT census data (red X marks the spot) geom_point( data = stations_for_map %>% filter(!has_census), aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat), color = "red", size = 1, shape = 4, stroke = 1.5 ) + labs( title = "Philadelphia Median Household Income by Census Tract", subtitle = "Indego stations shown (RED = no census data match)", caption = "Red X marks indicate stations that didn't join to census tracts" ) + mapTheme ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](week11_inclass_and_lab5_files/figure-html/join_census_to_stations-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ### Dealing with missing data ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Identify which stations to keep valid_stations <- stations_census %>% filter(!is.na(Med_Inc)) %>% pull(start_station) # Filter trip data to valid stations only indego_census <- indego %>% filter(start_station %in% valid_stations) %>% left_join( stations_census %>% select(start_station, Med_Inc, Percent_Taking_Transit, Percent_White, Total_Pop), by = "start_station" ) ``` ::: ## Get Weather Data ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(httr2) weather_raw <- read_csv( paste0( "https://mesonet.agron.iastate.edu/cgi-bin/request/asos.py?", "station=PHL&data=tmpf&data=p01i&data=sknt", "&year1=2025&month1=1&day1=1", "&year2=2025&month2=3&day2=31", "&tz=UTC&format=onlycomma&latlon=no&direct=no" ), na = c("", "NA", "M", "T") # M = missing, T = trace precip in ASOS data ) weather_processed <- weather_raw %>% filter(!is.na(valid)) %>% mutate( interval60 = floor_date(ymd_hms(valid), unit = "hour"), Temperature = as.numeric(tmpf), Precipitation = as.numeric(p01i), Precipitation = replace_na(Precipitation, 0), Wind_Speed = as.numeric(sknt) ) %>% filter(!is.na(interval60)) %>% group_by(interval60) %>% summarize( Temperature = mean(Temperature, na.rm = TRUE), Precipitation = sum(Precipitation, na.rm = TRUE), Wind_Speed = mean(Wind_Speed, na.rm = TRUE) ) %>% mutate( Temperature = ifelse(is.nan(Temperature), NA, Temperature), Wind_Speed = ifelse(is.nan(Wind_Speed), NA, Wind_Speed) ) weather_complete <- weather_processed %>% complete(interval60 = seq(min(interval60), max(interval60), by = "hour")) %>% fill(Temperature, Precipitation, Wind_Speed, .direction = "down") summary(weather_complete %>% select(Temperature, Precipitation, Wind_Speed)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Temperature Precipitation Wind_Speed Min. :10.00 Min. :0.00000 Min. : 0.000 1st Qu.:30.00 1st Qu.:0.00000 1st Qu.: 5.231 Median :37.00 Median :0.00000 Median : 8.154 Mean :38.35 Mean :0.02514 Mean : 8.863 3rd Qu.:46.00 3rd Qu.:0.00000 3rd Qu.:11.902 Max. :78.00 Max. :5.23000 Max. :28.000 ``` ::: ::: ### Visualize Weather Patterns ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(weather_complete, aes(x = interval60, y = Temperature)) + geom_line(color = "#3182bd", alpha = 0.7) + geom_smooth(se = FALSE, color = "red") + labs( title = "Philadelphia Temperature - Q1 2025", subtitle = "Winter to early spring transition", x = "Date", y = "Temperature (°F)" ) + plotTheme ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](week11_inclass_and_lab5_files/figure-html/visualize-weather-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ## Aggregate Trips to Station-Hour Level ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Count trips by station-hour trips_panel <- indego_census %>% group_by(interval60, start_station, start_lat, start_lon, Med_Inc, Percent_Taking_Transit, Percent_White, Total_Pop) %>% summarize(Trip_Count = n()) %>% ungroup() # How many station-hour observations? nrow(trips_panel) # How many unique stations? length(unique(trips_panel$start_station)) # How many unique hours? length(unique(trips_panel$interval60)) hist(trips_panel$Trip_Count) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](week11_inclass_and_lab5_files/figure-html/aggregate-trips-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ## Create Complete Panel Structure Not every station has trips every hour. We need a **complete panel** where every station-hour combination exists (even if Trip_Count = 0). ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Calculate expected panel size n_stations <- length(unique(trips_panel$start_station)) n_hours <- length(unique(trips_panel$interval60)) expected_rows <- n_stations * n_hours cat("Expected panel rows:", format(expected_rows, big.mark = ","), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Expected panel rows: 526,750 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("Current rows:", format(nrow(trips_panel), big.mark = ","), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output with 0 mutate(Trip_Count = replace_na(Trip_Count, 0)) glimpse(study_panel) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Rows: 526,750 Columns: 3 $ interval60 2025-01-01, 2025-01-01, 2025-01-01, filter(!is.na(lag1day)) cat("Rows after removing NA lags:", format(nrow(study_panel_complete), big.mark = ","), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Rows after removing NA lags: first(start_station)) %>% head(168) # One week # Plot actual vs lagged demand ggplot(example_station, aes(x = interval60)) + geom_line(aes(y = Trip_Count, color = "Current"), linewidth = 1) + geom_line(aes(y = lag1Hour, color = "1 Hour Ago"), linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.7) + geom_line(aes(y = lag1day, color = "24 Hours Ago"), linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.7) + scale_color_manual(values = c( "Current" = "#08519c", "1 Hour Ago" = "#3182bd", "24 Hours Ago" = "#6baed6" )) + labs( title = "Temporal Lag Patterns at One Station", subtitle = "Past demand predicts future demand", x = "Date-Time", y = "Trip Count", color = "Time Period" ) + plotTheme ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](week11_inclass_and_lab5_files/figure-html/lag_correlations-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ## TEMPORAL TRAIN/TEST SPLIT ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Split by week # Q1 has weeks 1-13 (Jan-Mar) # Train on weeks 1-9 (Jan 1 - early March) # Test on weeks 10-13 (rest of March) # Which stations have trips in BOTH early and late periods? early_stations <- study_panel_complete %>% filter(week < 10) %>% filter(Trip_Count > 0) %>% distinct(start_station) %>% pull(start_station) late_stations <- study_panel_complete %>% filter(week >= 10) %>% filter(Trip_Count > 0) %>% distinct(start_station) %>% pull(start_station) # Keep only stations that appear in BOTH periods common_stations <- intersect(early_stations, late_stations) # Filter panel to only common stations study_panel_complete <- study_panel_complete %>% filter(start_station %in% common_stations) # NOW create train/test split train <- study_panel_complete %>% filter(week < 10) test <- study_panel_complete %>% filter(week >= 10) cat("Training observations:", format(nrow(train), big.mark = ","), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Training observations: 352,240 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("Testing observations:", format(nrow(test), big.mark = ","), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Testing observations: 153,748 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("Training date range:", min(train$date), "to", max(train$date), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Training date range: 20090 to 20151 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("Testing date range:", min(test$date), "to", max(test$date), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Testing date range: 20152 to 20178 ``` ::: ::: ## BUILD PREDICTIVE MODELS We'll build 5 models with increasing complexity to see what improves predictions. ### Model 1: Baseline (Time + Weather) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Create day of week factor with treatment (dummy) coding train <- train %>% mutate(dotw_simple = factor(dotw, levels = c("Mon", "Tue", "Wed", "Thu", "Fri", "Sat", "Sun"))) # Set contrasts to treatment coding (dummy variables) contrasts(train$dotw_simple) <- contr.treatment(7) # Now run the model model1 <- lm( Trip_Count ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature + Precipitation, data = train ) summary(model1) ``` ::: The model uses Monday as the baseline. Each coefficient represents the difference in expected trips per station-hour compared to Monday - dow_simple2 = Tuesday.. **Weekday Pattern (Tue-Fri):** - All weekdays have positive coefficients (0.029 to 0.052) - Tuesday has the highest weekday effect (+0.052) - Weekdays likely benefit from concentrated commuting patterns **Weekend Pattern (Sat-Sun):** - Both weekend days have negative coefficients (-0.061 and -0.065) - This means FEWER trips per station-hour than Monday **Hourly Interpretation** Hour Coefficient Interpretation 0 (baseline) 0.000 trips/hour (midnight) 1 -0.018 slightly fewer than midnight ... 6 +0.151 morning activity starting 7 +0.276 morning rush building 8 +0.487 PEAK morning rush 9 +0.350 post-rush ... 17 +0.568 PEAK evening rush (5 PM!) 18 +0.389 evening declining ... 23 +0.034 late night minimal ### Model 2: Add Temporal Lags ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} model2 <- lm( Trip_Count ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature + Precipitation + lag1Hour + lag3Hours + lag1day, data = train ) summary(model2) ``` ::: ### Model 3: Add Demographics ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} model3 <- lm( Trip_Count ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature + Precipitation + lag1Hour + lag3Hours + lag1day + Med_Inc + Percent_Taking_Transit + Percent_White, data = train ) summary(model3) ``` ::: ### Model 4: Add Station Fixed Effects ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} model4 <- lm( Trip_Count ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature + Precipitation + lag1Hour + lag3Hours + lag1day + Med_Inc + Percent_Taking_Transit + Percent_White + as.factor(start_station), data = train ) # Summary too long with all station dummies, just show key metrics cat("Model 4 R-squared:", summary(model4)$r.squared, "\n") cat("Model 4 Adj R-squared:", summary(model4)$adj.r.squared, "\n") ``` ::: ### Model 5: Add Rush Hour Interaction ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} model5 <- lm( Trip_Count ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature + Precipitation + lag1Hour + lag3Hours + lag1day + rush_hour + as.factor(month) + Med_Inc + Percent_Taking_Transit + Percent_White + as.factor(start_station) + rush_hour * weekend, # Rush hour effects different on weekends data = train ) cat("Model 5 R-squared:", summary(model5)$r.squared, "\n") cat("Model 5 Adj R-squared:", summary(model5)$adj.r.squared, "\n") ``` ::: ## MODEL EVALUATION ### Calculate Predictions and MAE ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Get predictions on test set # Create day of week factor with treatment (dummy) coding test <- test %>% mutate(dotw_simple = factor(dotw, levels = c("Mon", "Tue", "Wed", "Thu", "Fri", "Sat", "Sun"))) # Set contrasts to treatment coding (dummy variables) contrasts(test$dotw_simple) <- contr.treatment(7) test <- test %>% mutate( pred1 = predict(model1, newdata = test), pred2 = predict(model2, newdata = test), pred3 = predict(model3, newdata = test), pred4 = predict(model4, newdata = test), pred5 = predict(model5, newdata = test) ) # Calculate MAE for each model mae_results <- data.frame( Model = c( "1. Time + Weather", "2. + Temporal Lags", "3. + Demographics", "4. + Station FE", "5. + Rush Hour Interaction" ), MAE = c( mean(abs(test$Trip_Count - test$pred1), na.rm = TRUE), mean(abs(test$Trip_Count - test$pred2), na.rm = TRUE), mean(abs(test$Trip_Count - test$pred3), na.rm = TRUE), mean(abs(test$Trip_Count - test$pred4), na.rm = TRUE), mean(abs(test$Trip_Count - test$pred5), na.rm = TRUE) ) ) kable(mae_results, digits = 2, caption = "Mean Absolute Error by Model (Test Set)", col.names = c("Model", "MAE (trips)")) %>% kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover")) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}

as.factor(month) + Med_Inc + Percent_Taking_Transit + Percent_White + as.factor(start_station) + rush_hour * weekend, data = train_q2 ) cat("Model 5 R-squared:", summary(model5_q2)$r.squared, "\n") cat("Model 5 Adj R-squared:", summary(model5_q2)$adj.r.squared, "\n") ``` ::: ### Model Evaluation ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} test_q2 <- test_q2 %>% mutate(dotw_simple = factor(dotw, levels = Adding demographics in Model 3 yields only marginal gains, while station fixed effects in Model 4 provide a small but meaningful improvement in R² (0.337) and RMSE (1.040), capturing baseline differences in busyness across stations. Interestingly, Model 5 slightly increases MAE and RMSE despite a higher R², suggesting the rush hour interaction may be overfitting to the training data and not generalizing well to the June test period. ### Visualize q2 model comparison ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(mae_results_q2, aes(x = reorder(Model, -MAE), y = MAE)) + geom_col(fill = "#3182bd", alpha = 0.8) + geom_text(aes(label = round(MAE, 2)), vjust = -0.5) + labs(title = "Model Performance Comparison - Q2 2025", subtitle = "Lower MAE = Better Predictions", x = "Model", y = "Mean Absolute Error (trips)") + plotTheme + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](week11_inclass_and_lab5_files/figure-html/compare_models_q2-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: # 4. This difference likely reflects the higher and more variable demand in spring and early summer, where warmer weather, recreational riding, and special events introduce greater unpredictability that the model struggles to capture. Both quarters show the same pattern of temporal lags providing the biggest single improvement, confirming that past demand is the most important predictor regardless of season. In Q2, additional complexity from demographics and station fixed effects yields smaller marginal gains, suggesting that Q2 demand is driven more by weather and temporal factors than by neighborhood characteristics. ## Space-Time Error Analysis ### Observed vs. Predicted ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} test_q2 <- test_q2 %>% mutate( error = Trip_Count - pred2, abs_error = abs(error), time_of_day = case_when( hour < 7 ~ "Overnight", hour >= 7 & hour < 10 ~ "AM Rush", hour >= 10 & hour < 15 ~ "Mid-Day", hour >= 15 & hour <= 18 ~ "PM Rush", hour > 18 ~ "Evening" ) ) p1 <- ggplot(test, aes(x = Trip_Count, y = pred2)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.2, color = "#08519c") + geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, color = "red", linewidth = 1) + geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "darkgreen") + facet_grid(weekend ~ time_of_day) + labs(title = "Q1 2025: Observed vs. Predicted", x = "Observed Trips", y = "Predicted Trips", caption = "Red line = perfect predictions; Green line = actual model fit") + plotTheme p2 <- ggplot(test_q2, aes(x = Trip_Count, y = pred2)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.2, color = "#6baed6") + geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, color = "red", linewidth = 1) + geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "darkgreen") + facet_grid(weekend ~ time_of_day) + labs(title = "Q2 2025: Observed vs. Predicted", x = "Observed Trips", y = "Predicted Trips", caption = "Red line = perfect predictions; Green line = actual model fit") + plotTheme p1 / p2 ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](week11_inclass_and_lab5_files/figure-html/obs_vs_pred_q2-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ### Spatial Error Patterns ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} station_errors_q2 <- test_q2 %>% filter(!is.na(pred2)) %>% group_by(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>% summarize( MAE = mean(abs(Trip_Count - pred2), na.rm = TRUE), avg_demand = mean(Trip_Count, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop" ) %>% filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon)) p1 <- ggplot() + geom_sf(data = philly_census, fill = "grey95", color = "white", size = 0.1) + geom_point(data = station_errors, aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat, color = MAE), size = 3.5, alpha = 0.7) + scale_color_viridis(option = "plasma", name = "MAE (trips)", direction = -1, breaks = c(0.5, 1.0, 1.5), labels = c("0.5", "1.0", "1.5")) + labs(title = "Q1 2025: Prediction Errors") + mapTheme + theme(legend.position = "bottom", legend.title = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"), legend.text = element_text(size = 9), plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5)) + guides(color = guide_colorbar(barwidth = 12, barheight = 1, title.position = "top", title.hjust = 0.5)) p2 <- ggplot() + geom_sf(data = philly_census, fill = "grey95", color = "white", size = 0.1) + geom_point(data = station_errors_q2, aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat, color = MAE), size = 3.5, alpha = 0.7) + scale_color_viridis(option = "plasma", name = "MAE (trips)", direction = -1, breaks = c(0.5, 1.0, 1.5), labels = c("0.5", "1.0", "1.5")) + labs(title = "Q2 2025: Prediction Errors") + mapTheme + theme(legend.position = "bottom", legend.title = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"), legend.text = element_text(size = 9), plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5)) + guides(color = guide_colorbar(barwidth = 12, barheight = 1, title.position = "top", title.hjust = 0.5)) (p1 | p2) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](week11_inclass_and_lab5_files/figure-html/spatial_errors_q2-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: **Interpretation**: Similar pattern to Q1. Prediction errors are spatially clustered and strongly correlated with average demand, with the highest MAE concentrated in Center City and the area just north of it where ridership is also highest. This is expected since high-demand stations experience greater absolute variability that the model struggles to capture. Outer neighborhoods with lower demand show lower MAE, but this may reflect a floor effect rather than better model fit. The spatial overlap between high-error and high-demand stations suggests the model systematically underpredicts at the busiest locations, which is the most operationally problematic pattern since those are precisely the stations that need accurate forecasts for effective rebalancing. ### Temporal Error Patterns ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} temporal_errors_q2 <- test_q2 %>% group_by(time_of_day, weekend) %>% summarize(MAE = mean(abs_error, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>% mutate(day_type = ifelse(weekend == 1, "Weekend", "Weekday")) temporal_comparison <- bind_rows( temporal_errors %>% mutate(Quarter = "Q1 2025"), temporal_errors_q2 %>% mutate(Quarter = "Q2 2025") ) ggplot(temporal_comparison, aes(x = time_of_day, y = MAE, fill = Quarter)) + geom_col(position = "dodge") + scale_fill_manual(values = c("Q1 2025" = "#08519c", "Q2 2025" = "#6baed6")) + facet_wrap(~ day_type) + labs(title = "Prediction Errors by Time Period: Q1 vs Q2 2025", subtitle = "When is the model struggling most?", x = "Time of Day", y = "Mean Absolute Error (trips)", fill = "Quarter") + plotTheme + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](week11_inclass_and_lab5_files/figure-html/temporal_errors_q2-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: **Interpretation**: For q2, PM Rush hours show the highest prediction errors for both weekdays (~0.97) and weekends (~0.93), indicating the model struggles most when afternoon demand peaks are at their most intense and variable in Q2. AM Rush and Evening periods also show elevated errors, while Overnight hours have the lowest MAE (~0.30) as expected given minimal and highly predictable demand. Notably, weekends tend to have slightly lower errors than weekdays during peak periods, likely because weekend demand is more gradual and spread out, making it easier to predict. The consistently high errors during rush hours suggest the model is missing features that capture day-to-day variability in commute intensity, such as weather interactions with peak hours or event-driven demand spikes unique to spring. ### Demographic patterns ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} station_errors_demo_q2 <- station_errors_q2 %>% left_join( station_attributes_q2 %>% select(start_station, Med_Inc, Percent_Taking_Transit, Percent_White), by = "start_station" ) %>% filter(!is.na(Med_Inc)) %>% mutate(Quarter = "Q2 2025") station_errors_demo_combined <- station_errors_demo %>% mutate(Quarter = "Q1 2025") %>% bind_rows(station_errors_demo_q2) p1 <- ggplot(station_errors_demo_combined, aes(x = Med_Inc, y = MAE, color = Quarter)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.4) + geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) + scale_color_manual(values = c("Q1 2025" = "#08519c", "Q2 2025" = "#6baed6")) + scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) + labs(title = "Errors vs. Median Income", x = "Median Income", y = "MAE") + plotTheme p2 <- ggplot(station_errors_demo_combined, aes(x = Percent_Taking_Transit, y = MAE, color = Quarter)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.4) + geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) + scale_color_manual(values = c("Q1 2025" = "#08519c", "Q2 2025" = "#6baed6")) + labs(title = "Errors vs. Transit Usage", x = "% Taking Transit", y = "MAE") + plotTheme p3 <- ggplot(station_errors_demo_combined, aes(x = Percent_White, y = MAE, color = Quarter)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.4) + geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) + scale_color_manual(values = c("Q1 2025" = "#08519c", "Q2 2025" = "#6baed6")) + labs(title = "Errors vs. Race", x = "% White", y = "MAE") + plotTheme (p1 | p2) / (p3 | plot_spacer()) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](week11_inclass_and_lab5_files/figure-html/errors_demographics_q2-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: **Interpretation**: For q2, All three plots reveal systematic demographic patterns in prediction errors. Higher-income neighborhoods show higher MAE, likely because wealthier areas tend to be in Center City where demand is more intense and variable. The negative relationship with transit usage suggests that stations in transit-dependent neighborhoods, typically lower-income communities, are actually easier to predict, possibly because their ridership patterns are more routine and less influenced by discretionary spring activity. The positive relationship with percent White mirrors the income pattern, reflecting the spatial overlap between race, wealth, and Center City location in Philadelphia. Together these findings raise equity concerns: the model performs worst in affluent, majority-white neighborhoods where Indego is already well-resourced, while performing better in lower-income, higher-transit communities that may have less margin for rebalancing errors. # 5. Feature Engineering & model improvement Three new features are added motivated by Q2-specific conditions: `perfect_weather` flags hours with temperatures between 60 and 75°F and no precipitation, capturing ideal biking conditions unique to spring. `semester_end` marks the final weeks of university spring semesters at Penn, Drexel, and Temple (April 21 to May 15), when student activity patterns shift significantly. `memorial_day` flags May 26th as a federal holiday with distinct recreational demand patterns. **I choose model 4 as my best model** because it achieves the lowest MAE (0.687) and RMSE (1.040) on the test set among all five models. While Model 5 adds a rush hour and weekend interaction term and achieves a marginally higher R² (0.341 vs 0.337), its MAE and RMSE both increase relative to Model 4, suggesting the interaction term overfits the training data and does not generalize well to the June test period. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} train_q2 <- train_q2 %>% mutate( perfect_weather = ifelse(Temperature >= 60 & Temperature <= 75 & Precipitation == 0, 1, 0), semester_end = ifelse(date >= as.Date("2025-04-21") & date <= as.Date("2025-05-15"), 1, 0), memorial_day = ifelse(date == as.Date("2025-05-26"), 1, 0) ) test_q2 <- test_q2 %>% mutate( perfect_weather = ifelse(Temperature >= 60 & Temperature <= 75 & Precipitation == 0, 1, 0), semester_end = ifelse(date >= as.Date("2025-04-21") & date <= as.Date("2025-05-15"), 1, 0), memorial_day = ifelse(date == as.Date("2025-05-26"), 1, 0) ) ``` ::: ## Model 6: Model 4 + New Features (OLS) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} model6_q2 <- lm( Trip_Count ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature + Precipitation + lag1Hour + lag3Hours + lag1day + Med_Inc + Percent_Taking_Transit + Percent_White + as.factor(start_station) + perfect_weather + semester_end + memorial_day, data = train_q2 ) cat("Model 6 R-squared:", summary(model6_q2)$r.squared, "\n") cat("Model 6 Adj R-squared:", summary(model6_q2)$adj.r.squared, "\n") ``` ::: ## Model 7: Model 4 + New Features (Poisson) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(fastglm) # Build model matrix for train X_train <- model.matrix( ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature + Precipitation + lag1Hour + lag3Hours + lag1day + Med_Inc + Percent_Taking_Transit + Percent_White + as.factor(start_station) + perfect_weather + semester_end + memorial_day, data = train_q2 ) y_train <- train_q2$Trip_Count # Fit fastglm model_poisson_q2 <- fastglm(X_train, y_train, family = poisson(link = "log")) cat("Poisson AIC:", model_poisson_q2$aic, "\n") ``` ::: ## Model Comparison ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Build test model matrix and track which rows are kept X_test <- model.matrix( ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature + Precipitation + lag1Hour + lag3Hours + lag1day + Med_Inc + Percent_Taking_Transit + Percent_White + as.factor(start_station) + perfect_weather + semester_end + memorial_day, data = test_q2 ) # Align columns X_test_aligned <- matrix(0, nrow = nrow(X_test), ncol = ncol(X_train)) colnames(X_test_aligned) <- colnames(X_train) common_cols <- intersect(colnames(X_train), colnames(X_test)) X_test_aligned[, common_cols] <- X_test[, common_cols] # Get row indices that model.matrix kept kept_rows <- as.integer(rownames(X_test)) # Create full prediction vector with NA for dropped rows pred_pois_full <- rep(NA_real_, nrow(test_q2)) pred_pois_full[kept_rows] <- as.vector(exp(X_test_aligned %*% coef(model_poisson_q2))) test_q2 <- test_q2 %>% mutate( pred6 = predict(model6_q2, newdata = test_q2), pred_pois = pred_pois_full ) mae_all_q2 <- data.frame( Model = c("1. ````` ::: ::: **Interpretation**: Adding the three new Q2-specific features in Model 6 provides a marginal improvement over Model 4, reducing MAE from 0.687 to 0.689, which is essentially no change in OLS. However, the Poisson model in Model 7 achieves the best MAE overall at 0.658, a meaningful improvement over Model 4's 0.687, confirming that modeling trip counts as a count variable better captures the distributional properties of the data than OLS. The slightly higher RMSE in Model 7 (1.052 vs 1.040) suggests the Poisson model reduces average errors but occasionally produces larger outlier predictions. Overall, the new features alone do not dramatically improve OLS performance, but the Poisson specification demonstrates that treating demand as count data is a more theoretically appropriate modeling choice for bike share prediction. # 6. Critical Reflection ## Operational Implications The final Poisson model achieves an MAE of 0.658 trips per station-hour, meaning predictions are off by less than one trip on average. For low-demand overnight and midday periods this is operationally acceptable, but during AM and PM rush hours where demand regularly exceeds 5-10 trips per hour, an error of 0.658 can still translate into meaningful rebalancing failures. The most problematic scenario is systematic underprediction at high-demand Center City stations during peak hours, where even a small shortfall in anticipated demand could leave stations empty before the morning rush ends. We would recommend deploying this system under the following conditions: as a decision-support tool rather than a fully automated rebalancing system, with human operators retaining override authority; with mandatory real-time monitoring of stations where predicted and observed demand diverge consistently; and with a buffer inventory policy at historically high-error stations to absorb prediction uncertainty. ## Equity Considerations The error analysis reveals a troubling spatial pattern: prediction errors are highest in wealthier, majority-white, Center City neighborhoods and lowest in lower-income, transit-dependent communities. While this might appear to favor equity, it reflects a different concern. Lower-income neighborhoods have lower average demand, so lower absolute errors may mask proportionally larger relative errors that still result in poor service reliability for communities that depend on bike share as a genuine transportation option rather than a recreational amenity. Deploying a system that systematically overprioritizes rebalancing resources toward already well-served Center City stations could deepen existing disparities in bike access across Philadelphia. We recommend implementing equity-weighted rebalancing targets that account for transit dependency and income levels, regularly auditing prediction performance disaggregated by neighborhood demographics, and involving community stakeholders in defining acceptable service levels across different parts of the city. ## Model Limitations The model assumes that historical temporal patterns repeat consistently, an assumption that breaks down during special events, extreme weather, or structural shifts in travel behavior. The Q2 test period in June showed higher errors than the training period, suggesting the model does not fully generalize across the seasonal arc of spring into summer. Key missing features include real-time station capacity data, bike lane network changes, points of interest density, and granular event calendars for venues like the Mann Center, Citizens Bank Park, and university campuses. The panel structure also assumes station-level independence, ignoring spatial autocorrelation between nearby stations that could be leveraged through spatial lag models. With more time, we would explore gradient boosting models such as XGBoost that handle nonlinear interactions between weather and time more flexibly, and incorporate a spatial weights matrix to capture the spillover effects of demand between neighboring stations. ------------------------------------------------------------------------
